video_title: Scaling Laws: Lawyering on the Frontier with Janel Thamkul
transcript: Janelle, welcome to Scaling Laws. >> Thanks so much for having me. It's an honor to be on the show. >> Oh gosh. Well, you're far too kind. And you have quite the impressive background. It used to be the case that people would say, "I want to be in the room where it happens." But I think anyone who's listening to this podcast would instead say, "I want to be in the lab where it happens." And you've been at Anthropic. You've been at Google Research. You've been in those rooms, in those labs where the future of AI policy and the future of AI governance is being shaped and we are fortunate to have you on to give us a bit of insight into what it looks like to find your way to those labs and to those spaces and then how to navigate the really tricky legal and policy questions that come up. So, I guess as a starting point, can you walk us briefly through your bio? How the heck did you end up on the frontier of this technology as a lawyer? >> Yeah. So, my path to where I am now was definitely nonlinear and what I would call non-traditional. Um, I uh initially went to art school for undergrad. Um, I have a creative side. I love making art. Uh, deep appreciation for the humanities. Uh, I love archaeology, too, which is a bit of a random side note. Um, but I went to art school and I went to UCLA for art school um for undergrad and UCLA at the time had the most the strongest conceptual art program in the country. And I think that was really um interesting for me because I had no exposure to conceptual art. Uh, and it really made me think, take a new perspective on how I thought about what creativity was, what art was. Was it just technical skill or was there kind of an intellectual component to it too? Um, and so went to art school, focused on painting, sculpture, photography, the fine arts. Um, but in the back of my mind and probably from my parents, uh, there was always the question of, well, what am I gonna do with this? how am I going to pay the rent? How am I going to make a living? Um, and so I rushed through undergrad in three years, got all the credits, went to summer school, um, graduated uh, in three years. Um, and at the end of the three years, I found myself unable to kind of give up on pursuing a creative uh, profession. And so I told myself I had one year to continue to explore. and maybe a trade was where I could find the sweet spot between creativity and paying rent. So, I went to fashion school in Los Angeles. Um, got a a a associates degree in fashion. Um, and then ended up working in uh LA designing clothes for indie celebrities like Christina Reachi and Beck, which was really fun and creative, but still unfortunately did not pay the rent. Um, so after doing that for a while, I got a um a new job at a more corporate design firm designing underwear for Hot Topic and J C Penney. Uh, and >> this is just it's it's so crazy already that I just I have to jump in because anyone who started thinking, "Oh, great. I'm going to hear from, you know, a former lawyer at Enthropic and Google." Already just the path that you're describing is so varied and so off the beaten path of what everyone would suspect would be the approach to these jobs. And I wonder just stopping to pause on the sort of creativity you had to experience in those jobs, to what extent was that ability to ask really open-ended questions something that you think maybe later informed your own legal practice and just the experience of not having a lot of confines outside of, well, I guess this underwear better fit the celebrity, but you get to be pretty dang creative about how it looks. I'm I'm guessing I I haven't ventured into underwear fashion recently. >> Yeah. I mean, I think um my experience in in art school for sure made me question even what I my conception of creativity. So when I initially started art school, I thought art required some sort of sweat of the brow technical um technical excellence and labor. you know, you needed what was a painting, what was a good painting if it didn't take 10 hours time and some sort of sweat of the brow from the artist. And of course, conceptual art is doesn't require that. I remember one of my professors, one of his most famous sculptural pieces was a piece called Glues West of the Mississippi and it was basically a 2x4 that he poured glue on top of and it was, you know, in galleries and he was renowned for that and it blew my mind and I was actually very resistant to it in the beginning because I felt art required this, you know, something more than just an idea. Um, but I actually eventually came around to the idea that you can have art, you can create art in so many different ways. Art is not just a physical output um, and how much time you put into it. It's uh, it's the ideas that even go into the final output. And actually, now that I'm talking about this, I see so many parallels to AI, right, and AI generated art. So I think that really that experience even pre the existence of AI um gave me some of the perspective and the tools to think about you know what we're thinking about today. >> That's so cool to hear because I think that the notion of associating quality with the quantity of time you put into something is going to be an anacronistic idea I think sooner rather than later, right? of just oh well show me the work and then show me how much time you put into it may not be what we're actually looking for if we're confronting really timely questions and we're confronting novel questions it may be just that the most elegant simple solution is what's most meaningful and most impactful and so to have that experience I I I look forward to circling back on this but I want to get back to your journey so uh we've we've gone through art school we've been hanging out with celebrities doing uh their styling. We've been working on underwear and fashion. Uh what's what's the next chapter? >> Yeah. So, I I was at the I was designing underwear um for probably about a year and I had a I tend to have existential crises and so I had a uh pre-quarter life crisis where I was sitting in I remember exactly where I was. I was sitting in the my office at the design firm and I just asked myself, what am I doing to help people? Is this really helping people? I want to help a community I care about. And so I ended up quitting that job and I started volunteering at a domestic violence shelter for Asian women and children. Um, and that eventually led me to take a full-time role at the DV shelter. Uh, and I speak Thai, so I could help um Thai women and children who are fleeing imminent violence. Uh, and it was probably, I would say, one of probably the most fulfilling job I've ever had. I mean, I was driving a little minivan, picking up women and and their kids, fleeing abusers at the corner of La Sienna and, you know, in in Hollywood and then making sure they weren't being followed and taking them to the shelter and then signing them up for, you know, get getting the kids into school, signing them up for benefits. Um, everything, you know, the full suite of what it takes to get someone back on their feet and to give them a new life. I had a hand in in putting together and and helping with. Um, and so I I ended up working as a children's advocate at the DB shelter for about a year and a half. Um, until I eventually decided that the next way I could level up my ability to help this community was to get a law degree. Um, and that is what prompted me to go to law school. >> Yeah. Well, and I think that story is so important in terms of and apologies. I've been nerding out on the Pope's encyclical as of late. Uh, so we're talking in early June and, uh, Pope Leo refers back to Pope Francis's recommendation, which is go experience suffering. Go see suffering and see folks who are struggling with difficulties. And that's where you will see the need to be involved, the need to step up, the need to help out your community. And I think that we often find in our own ways opportunities to kind of be further and further away from that suffering and from that pain and that experience of seeing what does it mean to get back on your feet. You know, we say that as kind of a a shorthand expression, but you lived that experience of watching folks go from what I'm sure were some of their hardest, most difficult experiences and then hopefully I'm sure you have some very positive stories of them then flourishing. And so I know in this uh this was a long way of saying getting back to that question of what a lot of labs and a lot of AI policy people talk about. you know, I want to see AI lead to human flourishing, and then we kind of just drop the mic and don't really describe, well, what does that even look like in practice? And I'm I'm guessing, and again, I hope to get to this in more detail down the line, that your chance to see that firsthand of really experiencing people who were on the precipice of very hard times and then landing on their feet gives you a sense of what does it mean to actually lead to human flourishing. >> Absolutely. Um, and for what it's worth, I went to Catholic high school, Jesuit uh educated, so a lot of the Pope's encyclical resonates um with me even though I'm not uh Catholic. Um yeah, I mean I I find that uh in professional and personal moments of my life where I've been struggling the most, the way I gain perspective and um come out of it is by volunteering or working with people who uh need help. Um and it helps me reset and reenter. You know, what are my problems really? My my problems are not that big of a deal. And um it helps me reconnect with humanity and um human nature and people. Uh so I hope that I've always kind of done that throughout my life and so I hope that's what I've brought to my work as well. Um I love that you mentioned uh using AI to assist and advance human flourishing because that is kind of the the the policy position that I helped put together at Anthropic particularly in copyright and creativity. you know, AI should [snorts] um you know, we can go get into the copyright issues later, but the overall goal is that [clears throat] AI will uh the the best use of AI is one that doesn't displace humanity, but actually assists and enhances humanity and figures out a way to continue human flourishment in whatever it is, whether it's creativity or human beings finding purpose, which in our current culture oftent times is through their jobs or you know work identity. Um [snorts] and AI is disrupting that. So uh that's the lens I I try to bring. Uh and I I do think it's informed by my kind of random experiences or my my nonlinear path. Well, [clears throat] speaking of the nonlinear and then moving into the most linear of processes, which is law school, uh, walk us through your kind of legal education and how that inspired you to think about technology as a good vehicle through which to kind of drive and animate that additional human flourishing. >> Yeah. So, um, as I mentioned, I went to law school, um, motivated by the desire to help the community I had been working in. And, uh, in my 1L year, I worked at a legal, um, nonprofit organization providing direct services to the Asian-American community, mainly on issues like immigration, domestic violence, uh, elder abuse. And that was a really eye-opening experience. And I I share this with some self-consciousness because what I'd love to say is, "Wow, I I went there and it was so empowering and I I felt so great and I continued the work, but actually my experience was um not quite that. Uh I was a little disenchanted by the lack of resources at nonprofits to actually fully help the community I cared about. I remember I spent four I was trying to fill out um a a client's uh immigration application and I spent four hours trying to download adobe reader on completely outdated computers and I was just sitting there thinking this is so dumb. I could be serving four clients if we just had the right resources. um instead I'm doing this like how how how are we going to help people and how are we going to affect change at the same time I started to feel that um the law was not enough and so one example here was I had a client who was a Thai woman who had been trafficked over from Thailand she'd been promised a a job at a restaurant in Seattle when she arrived they took her passport um they forced her into sex trafficking and sex work and basically over the course of four or five years shipped her all over the United States to work in brothel. Um and eventually the the um trafficking ring was busted and I was helping her obtain a U visa which allows you to stay within the United States if you're helping law enforcement prosecute a crime. >> Wow. and we were able to get her the U visa and so that was a success and so she could work in the United States, but there was so much more she needed um because of being forced into sexual sex work. She had contracted HIV um and had a lot of mental health issues that needed support. Even though she could work, the job she found was being a hostess in a hostess bar. So, not quite, you know, like upleveling to a place where you're on your feet and you're you're kind of leaving that world behind and and makes sense why. Um, but there was so much more she needed that I couldn't provide just as just a lawyer. um that I felt it it didn't it didn't feel the same as working as a social worker where you can holistically help someone and connect them with things that they needed. At the same time, >> such a astute observation though in terms of uh your phrase of the law is not enough I think is such a important and simple statement for law school students, for lawyers uh and for anyone thinking about the law as a solution mechanism to keep in mind because the one of my favorite words and apologies to listeners that I bring this up way too frequently is jertification which is the process of turning everything into a legal issue, right? Where supposedly the answer will just be more law, right? And if we just had a better law or we had a better policy, then we would know that it would be solved. And there are limits to that, right? You know, we can expect that better laws, better policy can help solve or at least set up better institutions and better problem solving approaches, but ultimately there's going to have to be some communal aspect and some kind of all hands effort. And that can be amplified by technology, which I'm sure we're going to get to. But I just wanted to point out that that's such a important lesson that I think lawyers, especially when I'm talking to some of my students who come in and say, "I'm going to be a lawyer to solve X." Well, you might want to ask what what truly are you going to be able to do just with that JD? Exactly. Um, so yes, that that was a really eye- openening perspective for me. Um, and at the same time, I also realized that I had a strong interest in intellectual property from my art background. So, after law school, I went to a a private firm in San Francisco, Fellbron, and Martell doing IP and business litigation. Um, and that's where I encountered my first uh kind of platform liability DMCA case. I was uh represent defending a a file hosting platform. Um and that's you know that's where I got into the core tech issues of the time. Uh as well as managing you know international arbitrations and uh national products liability litigation. Uh it was really eyeopening experience. I learned so much. Uh I went from I feel like that experience took me from baby lawyer to oh you know a practitioner. Um and then around 2015 I decided that maybe the law firm wasn't the right path for me. Uh and I went in-house to Google. Um first as product council for Google Play. So really different type of product than what I work on today. um consumerf facing uh the regulatory legal landscape fairly established um and uh content protection because I was counseling Google Play books and Google Play movies and newsstand um and so uh that's also though where I started um learning about machine learning algorithms recommendation systems because we're trying to surface the most interesting content to each individual user and that's all powered by um AI and and machine learning. And then in 2018, the company realized that they needed a legal team to support Google research. Uh Google research at the time was the Google or that was developing AI emerging technologies to support all Google products and services as well as just the core state-of-the-art research community. And I thought that sounds cool. I think I know what this is from Google Play. I have a little bit of, you know, experience there. I want to go do that. But keep in mind, I have no STEM background. I have no technical background. I just am very curious, find it interesting, and want to go learn and am good enough that I can kind of figure it out as we go along. [laughter] Well, I mean that curiosity mindset, again, not to repeat too many buzzwords, but it is incredible at your various uh I'll use your own term, although I I' I've experienced some of them myself, existential moments of what the heck am I doing in this space and occasionally feeling that sense of boredom of well, you know, I've seen these issues, I've solved those problems or I've seen this fact pattern before. being excited is so important to being a good worker and also deriving meaning from your work and it's telling that you've always leaned into that and it's it's benefited you tremendously and I think that the uh willing to kind of raise your hand at Google which uh having been a former Googler myself you know even the effort of switching teams is nothing to uh uh you know under like underestimate it takes a lot of energy to move within these behemoth big tech companies. And so to find your path to these new and novel and exciting areas is is really telling to uh the extent to which you champion that curiosity mindset. >> Absolutely. I think the throughine uh across every role I've had or everything I've pursued has been um you know, do I find it interesting? Do I find it intellectually stimulating? and or does it help a community I care about and does it create some sort of impact to the world or society or a community I care about? And if [snorts] you just follow that each step of the way makes sense. Even if when you look at the whole picture, you're like, how did you go from art and underwear to Frontier AI? [laughter] I I got to say I I was uh in the back of my mind right now just thinking, huh, should I title this from underwear to AI? Will will she be okay with that or is that just too on the nose? Uh we we can talk about that uh uh in post. But thinking also about your willingness to learn too I is really important because you know machine learning is not easy to gro and to dive into. And so your willingness as a lawyer without that STEM background to say I'm going to go learn from these engineers at Google who are top of the world best of the best in some of these fields. Can you talk about how as part of Google Play or just part of Google generally you went about your own sort of education in these technical aspects of the job? >> Absolutely. So um I think you have to just carry a low ego uh curiosity mindset and people can feel it. Um, and having a low ego, I don't know, coming coming to an issue or coming to a problem with I don't know, help me understand so I can help us find a path forward comes across and people feel it and they understand what you're trying to do. And then they want to help. And oftentimes, I mean, I love working with researchers. They are my favorite client group. Um, I've been told that I I've been called the researcher whisperer. Um because sometimes lawyers can can come into conflict with researchers. They're speaking two different languages. They don't fully understand each other. Back at Google, they would pull me in. I would do whatever it is people think I do. And then suddenly we're all like path forward on the right track. And I think what I bring is um wanting to understand and conveying a sense of I am trying to help you achieve your goal and and get the thing done that you want done because I agree it's really cool and I love it too. Um and if that comes across then they want to help teach you. They want to help educate you. They'll take the time. Um, and of course you have to have an open mind and absorb it. You can't come into a conversation or come into a problem already knowing that you know the answer the outcome here because then why are we even having the conversation? >> Yeah. Um but if you if you bring that attitude, you can't go wrong and you will build so much trust and incredible relationships with um you know people. I just love uh researchers and engineers because I I love being around people who are creating and um I think you know researchers and engineers are people who are building creating developing something new into the world that ideally is solving a problem or helping helping someone. Um and so yeah, it's just kind of a natural draw. Uh, and then once they're willing to help teach you, you know, I don't think I mean it's great if lawyers have a STEM background, uh, because maybe it helps with vocabulary and it takes less um, getting up to speed, but your role as a lawyer, as a non-technical person, is to be that translation layer between a highly technical group and non-technical people like policy makers or >> regulators. ers and if you um if you focus too much on the technical aspects then I think you start to lose the ability to translate in the other direction that is absolutely critical for unblocking or advancing you know what you're trying to achieve and what rings true throughout your experiences too is that having a belief in the value and meaning of the technology as something that's actually going to benefit people and serve communities and do good is a really important unlock for then going into those rooms and saying, "Hey, uh, researcher X or Y, I do want to help you, right? Because I see the broader picture of what is the mission we're actually trying to achieve." And when you're all bought into that vision and have a sense that what you're doing matters and what you're doing is going to benefit other people, then I feel like you start to ask better and deeper questions and you start to walk back that ego because you recognize that the ultimate goal isn't proving that you're just as smart as the researchers because speaking from my own personal experience, I will never be uh as as smart as these folks. But to then say, "Hey, help me get you to this important outcome that we're both excited by." Uh, I think that's kind of an underappreciated lesson in law school and in the legal profession generally, which I think as lawyers and as uh policy practitioners, not losing sight of the fact that you should be outcome oriented and you should be asking frequently, am I actually improving the lives of folks? And if I'm not, well, then that should give you pause in the first place. But assuming you are, well, now you have so much more reason to, you know, put in those long hours, show up at the office even longer, and subvert your own ego to ask those better questions. >> Absolutely. Um, you know, I think, uh, what I found that really interesting as I reflected on my progression through, uh, sorry, one second, Monty, the last day of school was yesterday. I've got kids running around. >> Oh, nice. You're good. You're good. >> Um, so absolutely. Uh, one of the things I've reflected on throughout my career actually is that as lawyers get more senior either at, you know, in-house or at law firms, it becomes a lot harder to go in with an open mindset. I mean, I think you probably feel like there's an expectation that you already know stuff because you're senior or you've been in the space for so long and it can start to cloud your um openness to admitting you don't know can can this person explain this to you? And you know at Anthropic I built out what we called the frontier legal team and I would have moments where you know I realized wait a minute the last time we were talking about how we do this training I thought it was this but I think it's evolved and my new team member has the ability that has the hall pass to say hey could you teach me how we do this now because they're new they're getting up to And I felt a sense of reluctance to admit that because I'd been here for over 3 years and I've been working on this for so long and you know feels weird to admit like oh could you could you teach me something that I think you think I should already know. Um so that's one thing I think lawyers need to be mindful of. uh which is not falling into that feeling that you know you're not allowed to ask questions anymore just because you're senior just because you've been somewhere for so long. This technology is changing so rapidly. The way it's being developed is changing daily, weekly, monthly. Um and you you constantly have to be in a place where there's no ego, there's no self-consciousness. um and and you're in constant communication with the researchers and engineers to understand, you know, what are you doing today? Well, and so I want to dive into what it's like to govern in a space that and litigate and think about legal issues uh in a space where there aren't clear precedents and there aren't easy answers and there aren't known frameworks because both with respect to legal education and policy generally I think a lot of us suffer from uh you know that familiarity bias of well whatever the future is going to look like. It's probably going to look something like the past and so we should just borrow from solutions that have been tried or institutional uh setups that we're familiar with and that'll probably work out. And yet, as you're noting and as we've seen um from Anthropic, from OpenAI, uh they've recently thrown out both labs uh very bold policy proposals uh including, you know, new schemes of uh wealth distribution, new mechanisms for uh uh thinking through the economic transition we're experiencing. And those are the sort of bold, dare I say, wild ideas in some contexts that lawyers, I think, and policy makers, I think, kind of feel innately uncomfortable with. And so I'd love to hear more about what were you doing with the Frontier Council team because that's probably not a team anyone's ever heard of. Uh, and how did that sort of willingness to consider the weird really help you out in that capacity? Um so you know to some extent I don't think Frontier Council is completely novel. I mean you know kind of circling back to my path uh my time at Google research was what Frontier Council does today. A large part of what Frontier Councils today uh Frontier Council does today. Um everything from how do you develop all different types of AI technologies. I mean, I covered facial recognition back when facial recognition was the big thing in AI, um, computer vision, ondevice processing, ambient sensing, I think blockchain was in there for some period of time. Uh, you know, the whole gamut of emerging technologies. Um, and you know, to your point about relying on precedent, you can't kind of just copy paste what's happened before. To some extent, the law is that, right? pre you know you you figure out what the risk is and how to mitigate it based on legal precedent in AI for copyright in particular Google books is you know seinal precedent that you have to know and we're seeing it play out uh in ways that we anticipated even preacht um and so I think it's a balance um you're you have to know enough about what's come before in order to steer your teams in the direction of you know th this is how this will likely play out. Here's where we can push the boundaries of risk. Here's where we need to draw the line because we've seen this in some shape or form before and it didn't go well. Um so that is I think the skill set of an emerging technologies lawyer which is you have to know enough but you can't be too wedded to it. You have to be willing to push the boundaries but you can't just yolo push the boundaries. you have to know, you know, what the what the constraints are and analogize to them. And so that's what I did uh you know, pre2023, pre-Chat GPT for many many years kind of I would say more in the background of things because AI was not at the forefront of public sentiment in the way that it is now. Um and then yeah came to Anthropic and left Google in 2023 came to Anthropic company was about a hundred people at the time. Uh I helped um build out the legal function. Um essentially building out all the different orgs, the commercial legal team, product council, litigation and regulatory. I of course was counseling the frontier development and then um I think at some point I was employment counsel and prosecuting patents with the help of outside counsel too before we brought in people who actually [laughter] were better positioned to do it. Um and then scaled the legal team uh into to a place where my main focus became the Frontier AI Council team. Um and so you know I think Frontier AI Council has to be creative. Um they have to balance precedent with pushing the boundaries and understanding the technology so that you can help steer it. You don't want to block it. Um because then we should all just go home and not build anything. But you need to resp you know steer it in a responsible way and put what the teams are trying to do in the pos the the best position it can possibly be in from a legal regulatory and ethical perspective too. Um what's been exciting uh beyond just kind of the doctrinal analysis around how can you use data, what are the copyright issues, what are the privacy issues has been the opportunity to work on more kind of cutting edge wacky uh so to speak issues. Um I think facial recognition was kind of like that at Google. Um I also was the lawyer who had to support um some of the early model sentience questions at Google. Um you know I think you'll remember from 2019 the headlines about the Google AI engineer thinking Google's models were sentient. So that was my first experience um dealing with these these issues and it you know gave me a lot of perspective about how we need to think about AI generally um AI in the hands of vulnerable communities and vulnerable populations or just people in vulnerable states. Um and then at anthropic I've had the privilege to think about uh model personhood, legal personhood for technology. um uh you know impact on society, rule of law and AI's impact on rule of law and concentration of power. All pretty meaty uh issues um that are intellectually fascinating and also hugely impactful. >> Wow. Well, when a lot of folks at labs tell me, "Oh, you know, I've been there for uh 3 months, so that's basically 18 months, or I've been there for 3 years, so, you know, that's the equivalent of a decade." Sometimes I'm like, "Well, I guess I believe you." In your case, I most certainly believe you because the number of hats you've worn, the teams you've set up, and all of the novel questions you've been exposed to certainly would be something that I'm sure you felt like you have lived many professional lives in the span of just a few years. But with respect to that idea of, you know, we hear folks in the AI community commonly say the phrase, go touch grass, right? you've been online too long. You've been prompting uh for too long. Go just actually experience the real world. What I like about your perspective is yes, go touch grass, but also go talk to people because I think that what gets lost in a lot of the AI governance conversations is the actual impact on people and not people who are hanging out in San Francisco or in DC or at least not uh you know in the labs in San Francisco or uh in the governing halls in DC. You know, this is a very weird moment for a heck of a lot of people. And if you don't have a background in talking to folks who have been through difficult times, then you may lose sight of what it is you're actually doing all this hard work for. And so I wonder for listeners who are thinking, "Okay, Janelle's very inspiring. She's creative. She's hardworking. She's been in so many fascinating positions. What are some of the big questions that you wish people were paying more attention to? And now obviously we see that there's everyone's becoming an AI expert now and uh you know everyone's a builder on LinkedIn and so on and so forth. So there's a lot of content out there but all that content withstanding what are the things that you wish people were talking about in more detail? Um, so I I am a very pragmatic person and I [snorts] think there are very lofty uh uh futuristic ideas out there that I find intellectually stimulating and challenging and love to work on. Um, but I always bring it back to, you know, how is this going to affect my neighbor or how is this going to affect um, folks in, you know, the Midwest who aren't in this AI bubble that we're in on the West Coast? And is this net good for them? And, you know, how do they feel about it? I think right now there's a lot of fear around AI, a lot of negative sentiment, particularly around job displacement. And it doesn't help that we're seeing headlines in the news about big tech companies laying off tens of thousands of people. Um may maybe or maybe not due to AI automation. And so I think you know I would like to see more progress around societal solutions, safety nets for the job displacement issue. I think there's the I don't want to call it cookie cutter but it's you know standard playbook. Oh, upskilling, uh, you know, re, you know, re-education so that people understand how to use AI. To what end, you know, is are are you going to have the, you know, um person who just got laid off uh go use AI to build a business or, you know, what what what are we what is the value and the benefit you're trying to bring back to the people who are currently being impacted by this transformation? Um, and how do you actually give it to them? That's a question that I think we are behind on answering and um social safety net systems uh programs that we're behind on building. Uh so I'm very worried about that. I'm also worried about the environment. Um there's the models, there's the technology. It's all very exciting. It's, you know, mind-blowing what the models can do, but it all relies on supply chain inputs that are uh that have significant impacts to communities, to environments, to ecosystems. Um, and I am a little worried that we are behind on clean energy, uh, and having the right environmental rules and regulations, uh, to balance the need for more energy to power all this stuff uh, against the impact on the climate and communities where the energy has to come from. Um, so those are probably my two immediate main concerns I think. Well, actually I've got many concerns [laughter] or many thoughts, but the other one probably is um the impact of AI on cognitive uh work. And it's not just cognitive work for people who already know how to write or lawyers who already know how to think critically. I'm worried about the use of AI for middle schoolers or high schoolers, people who are in pro like in places where they're trying to learn how to do cognitive thinking. Um, and whether this technology is going to displace that and then what the long-term impact of that is. Um so yeah many many societal kind of like less less futuristic more pragmatic you know uh immediate uh concerns about how the technology is going to impact society. >> Yeah. But I think that it's important to flag as you noted that doing kind of policy by streetlight is what I'll call it. Right. when the folks who say, "Oh, I couldn't find my keys." Well, they were only looking under the street light because that's the easiest spot to try to find your keys. Uh, in the same way policy making by whatever idea is just right in front of you, right? Like the oh, retraining and upskilling because yes, uh, surely that if I just say those buzzwords enough, you know, magical jobs will appear, but we have to be more creative than that and that's that's difficult for a lot of folks. But I do love this research agenda and I think for listeners here you go you have your assignment uh from Janelle. Good luck. Uh go get after it. But I wanted to close on this question of AI and creators because you are someone who's worn both hats among many other hats in terms of thinking about the creative community litigating and experiencing some of these issues. You mentioned Google books. uh obviously you have uh close familiarity uh there and then being at Anthropic knowing that Anthropics had its own run into its impact and use of copyrighted materials. So at a sort of meta level, how do you feel kind of personally about what AI means for our creativity and more generally for our uh creative community? Um so my my personal views um I think the current discourse around copyright and AI is a little misplaced. I think there are two actual underlying issues. One is um economic displacement. So creators, rights holders uh feel that they had um some sort of IP right value that is being used without proper remuneration. Um and so the desires to leverage copyright law in order to um ensure that the desired remuneration is provided. I don't think copyright law is the right solution. I think economic displacement is an issue that's broader than copyright. It's broader than creatives. It's going economic displacement is going to impact lawyers, financial uh professionals, you know, it's going to impact so many industries that copyright law is just this hammer for this one particular niche. And I I don't think it is aligned with how content is actually used in training a model. So I think you have to separate the economic displacement issue from the legal tools that you have on hand to address this problem. Second to the economic displacement issue which I think we should have a broader solution for. So not just specific to one industry but for everyone. Um there's the you question of how do you ensure the continuation of human flourishing and creative flourishing. Uh this kind of came up in um Judge Chabria's comments in the the Meta case um questioning whether you know Taylor Swift is going to be fine but you know this is going to impact all the future Taylor Swifts and make future Taylor Swifts not write songs um because AI will you know dilute the market for for that. Um, and I think that's the the thing we need to focus on, which is like how do we ensure that humans continue to want to create and are still incentivized to create and that artists, creators, content generators are um properly incentivized to to continue to continue to do so. I feel like I'm in a weird place as an artist because while I'm an artist, I also don't rely on it for my livelihood. And so for me, I can sit there and say, "Oh, I made this painting." And AI will never replace the feeling I get when I paint that painting. And it will never replace the ultimate output that I create. um because you know it is just me and it's physical and um it's it's my process that has the meaning and what it does for me whereas I think someone who is like making a living off of generating stock photography is in a very different position. Um, and I maybe that actually falls more into the first category of economic displacement, but I don't, you know, I do think there's an important piece that labs, society, government needs to play in um, ensuring that there's the right incentives to continue artistic creation and contribution to, you know, human culture through through art um, and creativity. So I don't know if that was like um >> yeah that was fantastic. No and I love that distillation of the issue because there's a tendency to just say the words copyright kind of again street uh street lamp policym or streetlight lawyering and just saying well copyright has to be the solution. It's what we've leaned on previously but instead we have to start asking bigger and broader questions. But uh Janelle, I know you have plenty of big bold projects to go pursue beyond this podcast, so we'll have to leave it there. But thank you so much for coming on Scaling Laws. >> Thank you so much, Kevin. It was a pleasure, and I'm honored to join you. >> All right. Thanks again. Talk soon. Bye-bye.
